6 min read
The ‘VIP’ idea is a concept more than a list of tactics. How we use
this idea in the SPP module is one expression. For this reason,
we’ll keep this section very high-level and abstract.
If you sell multiple products and services, it means you can
probably group customers into tiers. Which means you can
communicate with these tiers differently. In ways that are more
meaningful, for you and for them.
We’re going to share a story that André shared in ARM2 and 3,
which beautifully demonstrated this concept. Then we’ll share
some ideas about how you can use VIP tiers in your business.
Here is the story taken directly from ARM2/3:
How To Use The “VIP” Idea In
Other Creative Ways
If you’re a customer of ARM3, you’ll recognize this story.
But if you’re like me, the story never gets old.
My good friend, Brent Hall (from Multiplier Method fame) told
me a story back in 2006.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 9 --

It has stuck with me ever since.
The lesson has earned me a fortune over the years, so pay
attention…
This is how to increase the quality of your email list.
Here’s the story Brent retold told me during one of our meet-ups
in Las Vegas…
A great marketer in an obscure niche once sent an email to his list
with the subject line:
“The Most Valuable Tape in the World.”
The email contained a dramatic story about a series of audio
cassette recordings he made of a legend in this particular niche
who had retired a few years before.
The legend was known to everyone in the industry.
He created many of the standard practices, and he developed and
proved a number of theories which affected not only his
immediate industry, but many related industries as well.
This man was also elusive.
Nobody knew where he lived or how he spent his time.
Most of his discoveries were shared with the world through his
writing.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 9 --

He didn’t speak at seminars.
He didn’t do interviews.
He didn’t do private consulting.
There were also stories �oating around the community about
stuff he discovered that he held back because the consequences of
these being abused would be catastrophic.
This marketer then went on to say how one tape in particular
contained a secret that everybody wanted to know.
He listened to the tape over and over.
Then one day his wife removed the tape from the tape deck in his
car and left it on the dashboard.
Of course … parts of the tape subsequently melted.
However, he was able to �nd someone who managed to salvage
the entire interview.
Unfortunately, even though you can hear the interview fairly
clearly, a portion of the audio was somewhat distorted.
This email had all the classic elements of a great story.
There was a hook, there was con�ict, there was a hero and a
resolution.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 9 --

And there was a call to action…
Anyway, he did an absolutely fantastic job of building such
immense desire to own this tape.
Most people who read it would have spent hundreds of dollars to
get this tape.
Then, at the end of the email he said something like this:
“I’m not charging a penny for it.
Obviously, this tape is really going to help everybody who listens to it.
Because of the hassle of duplicating tapes, I’ve only got 50 to give away.
So it’s ONLY available to the �rst 50 people who respond to this email.
If you want it, send your �rst and last name and your mailing address
to me ASAP.
But here’s a fair warning: I don’t care if you’re the Queen of England …
If you miss out, you’re out of luck because I’m not making anymore
copies for anyone…”
What happened next?
That’s right, Sherlock…
My friend Brent sent in his details, but wasn’t really expecting
what happened next.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 9 --

The NEXT DAY FedEx knocked on his door and delivered a
package containing this tape.
He eagerly opened the package and found the tape attached to a
beautifully crafted letter.
The letter was hand signed in ink.
Now pay attention to the details…
�. He used FedEx.
�. The mailing slip on the package was �lled out by hand.
�. The letter was hand-signed in ink.
�. The cassette was attached to a letter with scotch tape.
�. There was a handwritten sticky note on the letter.
At the end of the letter he made an offer for a seminar seat for
“just” $5,000 to actually meet this legend in person.
Five grand is a lot of money but Brent was already predisposed to
spending the money and going.
The whole drama, from the �rst email through the tape and the
letter sold him…
The drama created an experience for him from the start, an
experience he was helpless to not want to be part of.
Brent listened to the tape several times…
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 9 --

And each time he went through it, it reinforced his decision to
pony up the money and attend the �ve-grand seminar.
He attended, of course.
The seminar was brilliant.
But the marketing lesson was just as valuable, if not more so.
Brent then went on to break down the metrics of the advertising
campaign, as he saw it (remember, this was mid-2000s)…
There was a 28 page sales letter.
That’s 14 double-sided pages.
Cost = $.07 14 = $0.98
Cost to duplicate the cassette tape = $0.50
Overnight FedEx = $15
You can probably add $0.50 for labor.
So that’s a total of $17 per mailing.
Multiply that by 50 letters.
That’s $850 for the campaign to just 50 people.
Can you imagine doing that?
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 9 --

Brent couldn’t when he �rst backed out the numbers in his head.
When you look at it from the surface, it simply didn’t make sense
to pay that amount of money to market to just 50 people.
It could well have been a total waste of marketing dollars if a
bunch of freebie seekers were the �rst people to respond to his ad.
About a year after this experience, Brent met this marketer again
at a seminar in Las Vegas.
He proceeded to give Brent the most valuable lesson of his
marketing career.
Not all your prospects or customers have the same level of
enthusiasm and interest in the solution you’re selling.
This guy showed Brent a very simple, but highly effective, and
extremely important system of relegating and promoting
customers through what he called, “tiers.”
In this marketers business, Brent was on the highest level because
he had previously spent a $997 on a home study course.
That was his most expensive product.
So Brent continually got valuable stuff in the mail from him.
It should go without saying that this tier system is completely
unspoken.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 9 --

You may want to read the story a few times, there are so many
insights packed in it.
So let’s look at some creative way to use VIP tiers. We’ll use our
business because it’s a frame of reference accessible to everyone.
We could set up an automation that noti�es us when someone
becomes a customer of all our core courses. We could then reach
out to them from our personal email account, and offer to jump
on Zoom or Skype for a chat.
It would be an opportunity for us to learn how someone is
thinking about implementing our ideas into their business, and
an opportunity for us to ‘think out loud’ and offer some
suggestions.
A call like this would be valuable to them and us. Win-win.
This is an expression of an idea that doesn’t scale well, which is
The customers and prospects never know that they are on a
certain level or that levels even exist.
Think about what I’ve explained in this email.
It completely shifted Brent’s perception, and consequently how he
approached marketing to people.
Same happened to me.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 9 --

not a bad thing. In fact, we’re always looking for ways to interact
with our audience that doesn’t scale. But if you have a business
that generates a lot of customers, this probably won’t be a viable
option.
You could do a version that scales better. Perhaps set up
automations that automatically reach out to tiers of people, that
delivers “gifts” that delight them. Instead of giving you speci�c
ideas, we suggest you read The Power of Moments: Why Certain
Experiences Have Extraordinary Impact by Chip & Dan Heath. It’ll
melt your mind with what’s possible.
We think you should execute some balance between doing things
that don’t scale and using automations that do scale.
Think outside of the box. Get creative. This is an opportunity to
delight, an opportunity to forge happy customers.
Please ask questions and leave comments below.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 9 --
